note_data.title: Manufacturing accounts
keyPoints: **Prime cost** comprises direct materials, direct labour and direct expenses only
**Factory overhead** includes all indirect costs incurred in the factory such as factory rent, depreciation of machinery, and factory supervisor wages
**Production cost** is calculated as prime cost plus factory overhead absorbed
**Work in progress** must be valued at production cost (prime cost + factory overhead) not selling price
Manufacturing account transfers goods to trading account at **production cost** which becomes cost of goods sold
**Under-absorbed overhead** occurs when actual factory overhead exceeds overhead absorbed and is debited to profit and loss account
**Over-absorbed overhead** occurs when overhead absorbed exceeds actual factory overhead and is credited to profit and loss account
Factory profit should be eliminated from closing stock valuation to avoid overstating profits

definitions.definition: The total of direct materials, direct labour and direct expenses incurred in manufacturing a product
All indirect costs incurred in the factory including indirect materials, indirect labour and indirect expenses
Prime cost plus factory overhead absorbed in manufacturing goods
Partly completed goods at the end of accounting period valued at production cost incurred to date

howToAnswer.instruction: Show all workings step by step. Start with opening stock + purchases/production - closing stock format
Use correct headings and format. Manufacturing account first, then trading account
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'

modelAnswer.question: Calculate the production cost per unit given: Direct materials $50,000, Direct labour $30,000, Factory rent $15,000, Units produced 2,000.
modelAnswer.answer: Prime cost = Direct materials + Direct labour = $50,000 + $30,000 = $80,000. Production cost = Prime cost + Factory overhead = $80,000 + $15,000 = $95,000. Production cost per unit = $95,000 ÷ 2,000 = $47.50 per unit.

commonMistakes: Never include selling expenses in factory overhead — these belong in profit and loss account
Students often forget to adjust for work in progress when calculating cost of production
Never value closing stock at selling price — always use production cost
Students confuse direct expenses with factory overhead — direct expenses are part of prime cost
Never add factory profit to production cost when transferring to trading account
examinerTips: Manufacturing accounts appear regularly in Paper 2 Section A
Always show the transfer from manufacturing account to trading account clearly
Remember to eliminate unrealized profit when factory transfers at above cost
Work in progress adjustments follow the same pattern as stock adjustments
formulas: Prime Cost = Direct Materials + Direct Labour + Direct Expenses
Production Cost = Prime Cost + Factory Overhead
Cost of Production = Opening WIP + Production Cost - Closing WIP
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Manufacturing account showing three main sections: Prime cost (direct costs), plus Factory overhead (indirect costs), equals Production cost transferred to trading account. Include work in progress adjustments at bottom.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (8 marks) — they asked students to prepare a manufacturing account showing prime cost, factory overheads, and cost of goods manufactured from given trial balance data
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P2 Q3 (6 marks) — students had to calculate work-in-progress adjustments and determine the factory cost of goods completed
This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P2 Q5 (10 marks) — candidates prepared a complete manufacturing account including opening/closing stock adjustments and transferred cost to trading account
This topic featured in O/N 2021 P2 Q2 (7 marks) — students calculated prime cost, added factory overheads, and showed the transfer to cost of goods sold

workedExample.question: ABC Manufacturing has the following information for the year ended 31 Dec 2023: Raw materials stock (1 Jan) $8,000, Raw materials purchased $45,000, Raw materials stock (31 Dec) $12,000, Direct labour $25,000, Factory rent $6,000, Factory insurance $2,000, Indirect materials $3,000, Work-in-progress (1 Jan) $4,000, Work-in-progress (31 Dec) $7,000. Prepare the manufacturing account. (8 marks)
steps: Step 1: Calculate raw materials consumed = Opening stock + Purchases - Closing stock = $8,000 + $45,000 - $12,000 = $41,000
Step 2: Calculate Prime Cost = Raw materials consumed + Direct labour = $41,000 + $25,000 = $66,000
Step 3: Calculate Factory Overheads = Factory rent + Factory insurance + Indirect materials = $6,000 + $2,000 + $3,000 = $11,000
Step 4: Calculate Factory Cost = Prime Cost + Factory Overheads = $66,000 + $11,000 = $77,000
Step 5: Adjust for Work-in-Progress = Factory Cost + Opening WIP - Closing WIP = $77,000 + $4,000 - $7,000 = $74,000
workedExample.answer: Manufacturing Account for year ended 31 Dec 2023: Raw materials consumed $41,000, Direct labour $25,000, Prime Cost $66,000, Factory overheads $11,000, Factory Cost $77,000, Add: Opening WIP $4,000, Less: Closing WIP $(7,000), Cost of goods manufactured $74,000
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct raw materials consumed calculation, 1 mark for prime cost, 2 marks for factory overheads breakdown, 1 mark for factory cost, 2 marks for WIP adjustments, 1 mark for final cost of goods manufactured

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused direct and indirect costs' — always classify factory supervisor wages as indirect costs (factory overheads), not direct labour
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently omitted work-in-progress adjustments' — remember to add opening WIP and subtract closing WIP to get the final cost of goods manufactured
Examiner Report 2021: 'Candidates mixed up raw materials and finished goods stock' — only raw materials stock appears in the manufacturing account, finished goods stock goes in the trading account
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must draw the T-account format showing: Manufacturing Account with debit side listing raw materials consumed, direct labour, prime cost subtotal, factory overheads breakdown, factory cost subtotal, opening WIP addition, and closing WIP subtraction; credit side showing cost of goods manufactured transferred to trading account. Include clear arrows showing the flow from manufacturing account to trading account.
note_data.quickRecap: Manufacturing accounts calculate the cost of producing goods in factories. Start with raw materials consumed (opening stock + purchases - closing stock) plus direct labour to get prime cost. Add factory overheads to get factory cost. Adjust for work-in-progress (add opening, subtract closing) to get cost of goods manufactured. This final figure transfers to the trading account as cost of goods sold for manufacturers.
